title: I let my ego override the market on what Ampush was worth.
cost: Turned down a $60–75M offer because I was sure it was worth more — then drifted for years, carrying the regret that came with holding on too long.
change: The market is never wrong about valuation. I separate what I want something to be worth from what it is worth, and I act on the signal instead of my attachment.

receipts.quote: Jesse's refusal to sell Ampush at a $60–75M offer because he believed the company was worth more led to years of drift and regret.

title: I spent years optimizing against failure instead of for what I actually wanted.
cost: So much energy went into 'I don't want to fail' that I never stopped to ask what winning was even supposed to look like — and built toward avoiding loss rather than a life I'd chosen.
change: The first question now is 'what do I actually want if I succeed?' Design for the win you want, not just the loss you fear.

receipts.quote: You end up spending a lot of time on 'I don't want to fail, I don't want to fail' — but the question a lot of people don't ask themselves is, well, what if I succeed? What do I actually want?

title: Zero-to-$30M was never the clean line it looks like in hindsight.
cost: Client losses, real personal strain, and moments the business genuinely could have died — the parts the highlight reel quietly deletes.
change: I teach the struggle, not the sanitized arc, and I assume the next climb will be just as non-linear as the last one.

receipts.quote: The path from zero to ~$30M at Ampush was not a smooth curve — it involved periods of real struggle, client losses, and moments where the business could have failed.
